chapter_summary: Adam and Eve have two sons — Cain, who was a farmer, and Abel, who kept sheep. Both brought offerings to God. God was pleased with Abel's offering but not with Cain's. Cain became very angry and jealous. God warned Cain to choose right and not let sin win, but Cain did not listen. He hurt his brother Abel so badly that Abel died. God asked Cain where his brother was, and Cain lied. God punished Cain and sent him away. But even though Cain had done something terrible, God still gave him a mark to keep him safe. Later, Adam and Eve had another son named Seth, and people began to call on God again.
main_lesson: God wants us to control our angry feelings instead of letting them lead us to hurt others.

memory_verse.kjv: If thou doest well, shalt thou not be accepted? and if thou doest not well, sin lieth at the door. And unto thee shall be its desire, and thou shalt rule over it.
memory_verse.little_bible: God told Cain: 'If you do right, you will be blessed. But watch out — sin wants to rule you. You must choose to say no!'

parent_guide: This is a challenging chapter — it introduces murder into Scripture. For young children, focus on: (1) the importance of bringing our best to God, (2) choosing not to let jealousy and anger win, (3) God's grief when we hurt others, and (4) God's continued care even for those who have done wrong. Do not dramatize violence. The mark God places on Cain is a sign of mercy — even amid judgment, God does not abandon His creation. Use verse 7 as the key teaching moment: God gave Cain a warning and a choice before sin took over. This is a powerful lesson about self-control and the danger of unresolved anger.
application_for_children: When you feel jealous or angry, talk to God before you act. God will help you choose what is right — and choosing right always leads somewhere better.

verses.kjv: And Adam knew Eve his wife; and she conceived, and bare Cain, and said, I have gotten a man from the LORD.
verses.little_bible: Eve had a baby boy and named him Cain. She said, 'God has given me this little boy!'
verses.little_reader_adaptation: Eve has a baby boy. She says God gave him to her.
verses.meaning: Eve recognised that the gift of new life came from God — even after the fall, she looked to the LORD as the source of blessing and hope.
verses.memory_phrase: God gives the gift of new life.
verses.prayer: Thank You, God, for giving people to love and care for me.
verses.discussion_question: Eve said her baby was a gift from God. What is something you are thankful God gave you?
verses.family_discussion: How does recognising our children as gifts from God change the way we care for them?
verses.do_it_today: Tell someone in your family, 'I am glad God gave me you.'
verses.illustration_prompt: Eve holding a newborn baby with joy on her face, warm golden light filling a simple dwelling, Adam nearby smiling. Warm storybook watercolor style.

verses.kjv: And she again bare his brother Abel. And Abel was a keeper of sheep, but Cain was a tiller of the ground.
verses.little_bible: Eve had another son and named him Abel. Abel looked after fluffy sheep, and Cain grew food in the ground.
verses.little_reader_adaptation: Abel cares for sheep. Cain grows food from the ground.
verses.meaning: Both brothers had honest work. God values every kind of faithful labour. The difference between them would not be what they did but how they came to God.
verses.memory_phrase: Each one had honest work to do.
verses.prayer: God, help me do my work with care and a happy heart.
verses.discussion_question: Cain and Abel had different jobs. What is a job you like to help with?
verses.family_discussion: How can everyday work — like cooking, building, or farming — be done as an act of worship to God?
verses.do_it_today: Help with one chore at home today and do it as carefully as you can.
verses.illustration_prompt: Abel gently tending a small flock of woolly sheep on a hillside; nearby Cain kneels in a field of green crops. Warm storybook watercolor style.

verses.kjv: And in process of time it came to pass, that Cain brought of the fruit of the ground an offering unto the LORD.
verses.little_bible: After some time had passed, Cain brought some of the food he had grown and gave it as a gift to God.
verses.little_reader_adaptation: Cain brings food from his fields as a gift for God.
verses.meaning: Cain knew he should bring an offering to God — the instinct to worship is there. But the heart behind the gift matters as much as the gift itself.
verses.memory_phrase: We bring our gifts to God.
verses.prayer: God, help me give You my very best, not just what is easy.
verses.discussion_question: If you were going to give God a gift, what would you want it to be?
verses.family_discussion: Why does the heart behind a gift matter to God? How do we help children understand genuine worship?
verses.do_it_today: Draw a picture of something you want to give to God — your time, a kind deed, or your best effort at something.
verses.illustration_prompt: Cain standing before a simple stone altar with an armful of grain and vegetables, looking serious. Soft morning light, warm storybook watercolor style.

verses.kjv: And Abel, he also brought of the firstlings of his flock and of the fat thereof. And the LORD had respect unto Abel and to his offering:
verses.little_bible: Abel brought the best of his sheep — the very first and finest — as a gift to God. God was very pleased with Abel and his offering.
verses.little_reader_adaptation: Abel gives his best sheep to God. God is pleased.
verses.meaning: Abel gave his firstlings — the very best of what he had — showing a heart of faith and wholehearted devotion. God's acceptance was a response to Abel's faith.
verses.memory_phrase: Abel gave his very best to God.
verses.prayer: God, I want to give You my very best, just like Abel did.
verses.discussion_question: What does it mean to give your very best to someone? Have you ever done that?
verses.family_discussion: Hebrews 11:4 says Abel offered his gift 'by faith.' What does it look like to give to God in faith today?
verses.do_it_today: Do one thing today with your very best effort — a drawing, a kind word, or helping with a task.
verses.illustration_prompt: Abel kneeling beside a stone altar, offering a lamb with gentle hands; a warm glow of light above the altar shows God's pleasure. Warm storybook watercolor style.

verses.kjv: But unto Cain and to his offering he had not respect. And Cain was very wroth, and his countenance fell.
verses.little_bible: But God was not pleased with Cain's offering. Cain became very angry — his whole face showed how upset he was.
verses.little_reader_adaptation: God is not pleased with Cain. Cain gets very angry.
verses.meaning: Anger and a fallen face are outward signs of an inward problem — Cain's issue was not his job but the posture of his heart toward God. Anger at God's judgment reveals where our trust truly lies.
verses.memory_phrase: Cain's anger showed in his face.
verses.prayer: God, when I feel angry, help me talk to You instead of letting the anger grow.
verses.discussion_question: Have you ever been upset when something was not fair? What did you do with that feeling?
verses.family_discussion: How do we help children bring disappointment and anger to God rather than letting it fester?
verses.do_it_today: If something makes you upset today, stop and tell God how you feel before you do anything else.
verses.illustration_prompt: Cain standing with slumped shoulders and a scowling, downcast face beside his altar; the smoke from his offering drifts sideways. Warm storybook watercolor style.

verses.kjv: And the LORD said unto Cain, Why art thou wroth? and why is thy countenance fallen?
verses.little_bible: God came to Cain and asked gently, 'Why are you so angry? Why does your face look so sad and upset?'
verses.little_reader_adaptation: God asks Cain, 'Why are you so angry?'
verses.meaning: God did not ignore Cain's anger — He reached out with a question, inviting Cain to talk honestly. God meets us in our difficult emotions and asks us to examine them.
verses.memory_phrase: God asks why we are angry.
verses.prayer: God, thank You for caring about how I feel. Help me be honest with You.
verses.discussion_question: God asked Cain a question because He cared. What would you say to God if He asked you, 'Why are you upset?'
verses.family_discussion: God checked in on Cain before things got worse. How can families create space for children to talk about difficult feelings before they explode?
verses.do_it_today: When a big feeling comes today, try saying it out loud: 'God, I feel ___. Please help me.'
verses.illustration_prompt: A ray of warm light shining down on Cain who sits with a heavy heart; the light feels like a gentle presence asking a question. Warm storybook watercolor style.

verses.kjv: If thou doest well, shalt thou not be accepted? and if thou doest not well, sin lieth at the door. And unto thee shall be its desire, and thou shalt rule over it.
verses.little_bible: God said to Cain, 'If you do right, things will go well for you! But if you do wrong, sin is crouching at the door like a hungry animal, waiting to get in. You must be the one to stop it.'
verses.little_reader_adaptation: God says: do right and be blessed. Sin is waiting at the door — say no!
verses.meaning: God gave Cain a clear choice and a warning: sin is not unbeatable, but it must be actively resisted. This verse is the heart of the chapter — a call to self-control and right living.
verses.memory_phrase: Say no to sin at the door.
verses.prayer: God, help me choose what is right even when it is hard. Help me say no to the bad things that want to come in.
verses.discussion_question: If sin is like a hungry animal waiting at the door, what can you do to keep the door shut?
verses.family_discussion: God said Cain could 'rule over' sin — this means self-control is possible with God's help. How do we practise ruling over sinful impulses as a family?
verses.do_it_today: Think of one thing you are tempted to do wrong. Make a plan with a parent for what to do instead.
verses.illustration_prompt: A glowing doorway with a shadowy crouching shape near it; a child-like figure standing confidently in front of the door, choosing not to let the shadow in. Warm storybook watercolor style.

verses.kjv: And Cain talked with Abel his brother: and it came to pass, when they were in the field, that Cain rose up against Abel his brother, and slew him.
verses.little_bible: Cain called Abel out to the field. While they were there, Cain let his anger take over, and he hurt Abel so badly that Abel died.
verses.little_reader_adaptation: Cain hurts Abel, and Abel dies. Sin took over.
verses.meaning: Cain chose not to listen to God's warning. What began as jealousy grew into the world's first act of murder — a tragic picture of what unchecked sin does to relationships and to life.
verses.memory_phrase: Anger that grows leads to terrible things.
verses.prayer: God, I never want my anger to hurt someone I love. Please help me stop anger before it grows.
verses.discussion_question: Cain's anger kept growing because he did not talk to God. Who can you talk to when you feel very angry?
verses.family_discussion: This is the first death caused by sin between people. How do we explain to children that our choices can harm others — and why that grieves God?
verses.do_it_today: If you are upset with someone, instead of staying angry, go to a parent and talk about it today.
verses.illustration_prompt: Two brothers in an open field; one figure lying still on the ground, the other standing with head bowed in shadows. The scene is quiet and sad, no graphic detail. Warm storybook watercolor style.

verses.kjv: And the LORD said unto Cain, Where is Abel thy brother? And he said, I know not: Am I my brother's keeper?
verses.little_bible: God asked Cain, 'Where is your brother Abel?' But Cain lied and said, 'I don't know. It's not my job to look after him!'
verses.little_reader_adaptation: God asks where Abel is. Cain lies and says he does not know.
verses.meaning: Cain lied to God — which is impossible, since God knows everything. His dismissive question 'Am I my brother's keeper?' reveals a heart that refused responsibility for others.
verses.memory_phrase: We are meant to care for each other.
verses.prayer: God, help me always tell the truth, even when I have done something wrong.
verses.discussion_question: Cain lied to God. Why is it better to tell the truth even when we have made a mistake?
verses.family_discussion: We are called to be our 'brother's keeper.' What does it look like in daily life to take care of and look out for others?
verses.do_it_today: Do something kind for a sibling, friend, or neighbour today — be their 'keeper.'
verses.illustration_prompt: Cain standing with arms crossed, looking away from a beam of warm light that represents God asking him a question. Warm storybook watercolor style.

verses.kjv: And he said, What hast thou done? the voice of thy brother's blood crieth unto me from the ground.
verses.little_bible: God said to Cain, 'What have you done? Your brother Abel is crying out to Me from the ground. I hear everything.'
verses.little_reader_adaptation: God says, 'I hear Abel crying to Me. I know what you did.'
verses.meaning: God hears the cries of the wronged and the hurting — nothing is hidden from Him. This verse shows both God's justice and His deep care for every life.
verses.memory_phrase: God hears every cry for help.
verses.prayer: God, thank You that You hear when people are hurting. Help me care about others the way You do.
verses.discussion_question: God said He heard Abel. How does it feel to know God hears you when you cry?
verses.family_discussion: God's hearing Abel's blood is a picture of His justice. How do we raise children who understand that God sees injustice and cares — and calls us to care too?
verses.do_it_today: Think of someone who might be sad or hurting. Do one small thing to show them they are not alone.
verses.illustration_prompt: The ground with a soft red glow, and above it a gentle swirl of light rising upward toward the heavens, representing a voice being heard. Warm storybook watercolor style.

verses.kjv: And now art thou cursed from the earth, which hath opened her mouth to receive thy brother's blood from thy hand;
verses.little_bible: God told Cain, 'Because of what you did, the ground itself will no longer work well for you. Your sin has changed things.'
verses.little_reader_adaptation: Because of what Cain did, the ground will not help him anymore.
verses.meaning: Sin has consequences that reach beyond the person who sins — it affects creation, relationships, and the ability to flourish. The ground that Cain loved to farm now worked against him.
verses.memory_phrase: Sin always brings consequences.
verses.prayer: God, help me remember that wrong choices have hard results. Help me choose right.
verses.discussion_question: Cain's punishment was connected to the work he loved. What do you think that felt like?
verses.family_discussion: How do we honestly teach children that sin has consequences without removing the message of grace and restoration?
verses.do_it_today: Think of a choice you made that had a consequence. Talk with a parent about what you learned from it.
verses.illustration_prompt: Cain kneeling over dry, cracked earth; the fields around him look withered and grey compared to the lush green we saw before. Warm storybook watercolor style.

verses.kjv: When thou tillest the ground, it shall not henceforth yield unto thee her strength; a fugitive and a vagabond shalt thou be in the earth.
verses.little_bible: God said, 'The ground will not grow good things for you any more. You will wander from place to place and have no home.'
verses.little_reader_adaptation: Cain must wander and has no home. The ground will not help him.
verses.meaning: Cain was separated from the soil he worked and from a settled home — consequences that mirrored the relational separation his sin had caused. Wandering here represents both physical and spiritual displacement.
verses.memory_phrase: Sin takes us far from home.
verses.prayer: God, thank You that You are always home for me, even when I mess up.
verses.discussion_question: What would it feel like to have no home and have to wander around? What do you love about having a home?
verses.family_discussion: Cain's wandering is a picture of what sin does to us spiritually — it separates us from belonging. How does Jesus undo that separation?
verses.do_it_today: Thank God for your home today — say one thing you love about where you live.
verses.illustration_prompt: Cain walking alone down a long dusty path, hunched over, with no destination in sight; the sky is pale and vast above him. Warm storybook watercolor style.

verses.kjv: And Cain said unto the LORD, My punishment is greater than I can bear.
verses.little_bible: Cain said to God, 'This punishment is too heavy for me! I cannot carry it!'
verses.little_reader_adaptation: Cain says, 'This is too hard for me to carry.'
verses.meaning: Cain felt the weight of his punishment but did not express true repentance — he mourned the cost, not the wrong. Still, God listened and responded with mercy.
verses.memory_phrase: God hears us even when we are struggling.
verses.prayer: God, when things feel too hard, help me come to You instead of giving up.
verses.discussion_question: Have you ever felt like something was just too hard to deal with? What did you do?
verses.family_discussion: Cain cried out under his burden. How do we encourage children to bring their burdens to God — even when they have caused them themselves?
verses.do_it_today: Tell God one thing that feels heavy or too hard right now, and ask for His help.
verses.illustration_prompt: Cain on his knees, head in his hands, looking overwhelmed under a heavy grey sky; a faint warm light still glows in the distance. Warm storybook watercolor style.

verses.kjv: Behold, thou hast driven me out this day from the face of the earth; and from thy face shall I be hid; and I shall be a fugitive and a vagabond in the earth; and it shall come to pass, that every one that findeth me shall slay me.
verses.little_bible: Cain said, 'You are sending me away from my home and away from You, God. Now I will be all alone — and anyone who finds me might hurt me!'
verses.little_reader_adaptation: Cain is scared he will be alone and get hurt.
verses.meaning: The deepest pain Cain expressed was being hidden from God's face — separation from the presence of God is the ultimate consequence of sin. This foreshadows the great longing for restoration that runs through all of Scripture.
verses.memory_phrase: Nothing is worse than being far from God.
verses.prayer: God, please never let me be far from You. I want to always be near You.
verses.discussion_question: Cain was most afraid of being away from God. What do you think it would feel like to not have God near?
verses.family_discussion: How does Jesus' promise 'I will never leave you nor forsake you' answer Cain's deepest fear — and ours?
verses.do_it_today: Find a quiet spot and tell God, 'I am glad You are always with me.'
verses.illustration_prompt: Cain walking away from a warm, glowing light that represents God's presence; he glances back over his shoulder with a longing expression. Warm storybook watercolor style.

verses.kjv: And the LORD said unto him, Therefore whosoever slayeth Cain, vengeance shall be taken on him sevenfold. And the LORD set a mark upon Cain, lest any finding him should kill him.
verses.little_bible: God said, 'No one is allowed to hurt you.' And God put a special mark on Cain to keep him safe, even though he had done something terrible.
verses.little_reader_adaptation: God puts a mark on Cain to keep him safe. Even Cain is protected by God.
verses.meaning: Even in judgment, God shows mercy. The mark was not a brand of shame but a shield of protection — a stunning picture of grace extended even to the worst sinner.
verses.memory_phrase: God shows mercy even to sinners.
verses.prayer: God, thank You that You don't stop caring for people even when they do terrible things. Thank You for Your mercy.
verses.discussion_question: God protected Cain even after what Cain did. What does that tell you about how God loves people?
verses.family_discussion: God's mark on Cain shows that punishment and protection can exist together. How does this point us to the cross — where justice and mercy both happen at once?
verses.do_it_today: Think of someone who has been unkind to you. Ask God to help you be kind to them anyway, just like God was kind to Cain.
verses.illustration_prompt: A soft glowing mark on Cain's forehead — not scary, but protective, like a guardian symbol; Cain's expression is surprised and humbled. Warm storybook watercolor style.

verses.kjv: And Cain went out from the presence of the LORD, and dwelt in the land of Nod, on the east of Eden.
verses.little_bible: Cain left the place where God had been with him, and he went to live in a land called Nod, far to the east of Eden.
verses.little_reader_adaptation: Cain goes far away to live in a land called Nod.
verses.meaning: Cain departed from God's presence — the saddest journey anyone can make. Yet God had still provided protection. The land of Nod ('wandering') became Cain's home, reflecting the rootlessness sin produces.
verses.memory_phrase: Leaving God leads to wandering.
verses.prayer: God, help me always stay close to You and never walk away from You.
verses.discussion_question: The land where Cain went was called 'Nod,' which means wandering. What does it mean to wander?
verses.family_discussion: How do we stay close to God's presence in daily family life? What habits help us not drift away?
verses.do_it_today: Do something to stay close to God today — read a Bible verse, say a prayer, or sing a worship song.
verses.illustration_prompt: Cain's small figure walking into a vast, empty landscape under a wide sky; behind him in the distance glows the warmth of Eden. Warm storybook watercolor style.

verses.kjv: And Cain knew his wife; and she conceived, and bare Enoch: and he builded a city, and called the name of the city, after the name of his son, Enoch.
verses.little_bible: Cain's wife had a son named Enoch. Cain built a whole city and named it after his son Enoch.
verses.little_reader_adaptation: Cain has a son named Enoch and builds a city.
verses.meaning: Even outside of Eden, life continued and people built, created, and formed communities. This shows human creativity — a gift from God — at work even in a broken world.
verses.memory_phrase: God's gifts of creativity stay with us.
verses.prayer: God, thank You for giving people the ability to build and create. Help me use my gifts for good.
verses.discussion_question: Cain built a whole city! What would you build if you could build anything?
verses.family_discussion: Human creativity — building, naming, organising — reflects the image of God in us. How can we encourage children to use creative gifts in ways that honour God?
verses.do_it_today: Build or create something today — a tower of blocks, a drawing, or a LEGO creation — and thank God for creativity.
verses.illustration_prompt: A simple, sturdy city being built on a plain; Cain directing workers, with small figures raising stone walls under a clear sky. Warm storybook watercolor style.

verses.kjv: And unto Enoch was born Irad: and Irad begat Mehujael: and Mehujael begat Methusael: and Methusael begat Lamech.
verses.little_bible: Enoch had a son, and that son had a son, and the family kept growing — until a man named Lamech was born.
verses.little_reader_adaptation: Enoch's family grows and grows. Later Lamech is born.
verses.meaning: This genealogy traces the line of Cain through generations. Families and histories extend even from broken beginnings. God's world fills with people, though sin has also spread.
verses.memory_phrase: Families grow through the generations.
verses.prayer: God, thank You for my family — for the people who came before me and those who are here now.
verses.discussion_question: Can you name some of your grandparents or great-grandparents? What do you know about them?
verses.family_discussion: This is a good moment to share family history with children — where the family came from, faith stories passed down, and what you hope to pass on.
verses.do_it_today: Ask a grandparent or older relative to tell you one story about your family.
verses.illustration_prompt: A simple family tree illustrated as a real tree with leaves, each leaf bearing a small figure or name, roots deep in the ground. Warm storybook watercolor style.

verses.kjv: And Lamech took unto him two wives: the name of the one was Adah, and the name of the other Zillah.
verses.little_bible: A man named Lamech married two women at the same time. This was not the way God had designed marriage to be.
verses.little_reader_adaptation: Lamech has two wives. That is not how God designed marriage.
verses.meaning: Lamech's polygamy is an early example of sin distorting God's design for marriage, established in Genesis 2 as one man and one woman. As Cain's line continued, so did the pattern of departing from God's ways.
verses.memory_phrase: God's design is always best.
verses.prayer: God, help me always look to You to learn the right way to do things.
verses.discussion_question: We learned that God designed marriage in Genesis 2. Can you remember what His design was?
verses.family_discussion: God's original design for marriage in Genesis 2 was one man and one woman. How do we affirm God's good design for our children in a world with many different examples?
verses.do_it_today: Thank God for one example of love and care in your family.
verses.illustration_prompt: A stylised scene of a man standing between two women, the composition slightly off-balance, subtly reflecting that something is out of order. Warm storybook watercolor style.

verses.kjv: And Adah bare Jabal: he was the father of such as dwell in tents, and of such as have cattle.
verses.little_bible: Adah had a son named Jabal. He was the first person to figure out how to live in tents and take care of herds of animals.
verses.little_reader_adaptation: Jabal is first to live in tents and raise animals.
verses.meaning: Jabal pioneered nomadic life and animal husbandry. Even from a broken family line, God's gift of practical wisdom and industry continued.
verses.memory_phrase: People use their gifts to serve others.
verses.prayer: God, thank You for giving people different skills. Help me use mine to help others.
verses.discussion_question: Jabal figured out something new that helped many people. What is something you are good at that could help others?
verses.family_discussion: How can we help children see their unique skills and gifts as things God has given them to serve others and the world?
verses.do_it_today: Use a skill or talent you have to help someone in your household today.
verses.illustration_prompt: A cheerful scene of tents on a wide plain with cattle grazing nearby; a family moving their camp in a colourful, adventurous way. Warm storybook watercolor style.

verses.kjv: And his brother's name was Jubal: he was the father of all such as handle the harp and organ.
verses.little_bible: Jubal was Jabal's brother. He was the very first person to play a harp and make music — the grandfather of all musicians!
verses.little_reader_adaptation: Jubal is first to play music. Music comes from God's gift!
verses.meaning: Music is a gift God wove into humanity from the beginning. Even in a broken world, beauty, creativity, and song testify to the image of God in people.
verses.memory_phrase: Music is a gift from God.
verses.prayer: God, thank You for music! I love to sing and listen to songs. Help me praise You with music.
verses.discussion_question: What is your favourite song? How does music make you feel?
verses.family_discussion: Music has the power to shape the heart. What songs, hymns, or worship music do you want your children to grow up knowing?
verses.do_it_today: Sing or hum your favourite song today — or sing a worship song together as a family.
verses.illustration_prompt: A joyful figure playing a harp under a tree, with musical notes floating through the air like butterflies. Warm storybook watercolor style.

verses.kjv: And Zillah, she also bare Tubalcain, an instructer of every cutting instrument of brass and iron: and the sister of Tubalcain was Naamah.
verses.little_bible: Zillah had a son named Tubal-cain, who learned how to make tools and useful things out of bronze and iron. He had a sister named Naamah.
verses.little_reader_adaptation: Tubal-cain makes tools from metal. His sister is Naamah.
verses.meaning: Metalworking — the skill of shaping bronze and iron — emerged here. Like music and farming, craft and industry are expressions of God-given creativity, though tools can be used for both good and harm.
verses.memory_phrase: Skills can be used for good.
verses.prayer: God, help me use the things I am good at to make the world better, not worse.
verses.discussion_question: Tubal-cain made tools from metal. What tools do you use every day? Who made them?
verses.family_discussion: Human skills like metalworking, music, and farming can all be used to serve others or to hurt them. How do we help children direct their gifts toward goodness?
verses.do_it_today: Find one tool in your house and talk about what it is used for and how it helps people.
verses.illustration_prompt: A craftsman at a small forge, shaping a tool with careful hands; the glow of the fire lights up a focused, creative face. Warm storybook watercolor style.

verses.kjv: And Lamech said unto his wives, Adah and Zillah, Hear my voice; ye wives of Lamech, hearken unto my speech: for I have slain a man to my wounding, and a young man to my hurt.
verses.little_bible: Lamech boasted to his wives about hurting someone. Instead of feeling sorry, he was proud of it. Sin had grown even worse in Cain's family line.
verses.little_reader_adaptation: Lamech brags about hurting someone. Sin is growing worse.
verses.meaning: Lamech's boast shows how sin grows across generations — what began with Cain's jealousy has become pride in violence. This is a picture of moral decline when people move away from God.
verses.memory_phrase: Sin grows when we stop turning to God.
verses.prayer: God, help me never be proud of doing something wrong. Help me always say sorry and turn back to You.
verses.discussion_question: Lamech boasted about doing wrong instead of feeling sorry. Why is it important to feel sorry when we hurt someone?
verses.family_discussion: How do we help children cultivate a tender conscience — the ability to feel genuine sorrow for wrongdoing rather than pride or indifference?
verses.do_it_today: If you have hurt someone recently, say sorry to them today — in person, in a note, or in a drawing.
verses.illustration_prompt: A proud figure speaking dramatically to two figures beside him, arms spread; the scene has dark, heavy tones contrasting with the lighter scenes of music and craftsmanship. Warm storybook watercolor style.

verses.kjv: If Cain shall be avenged sevenfold, truly Lamech seventy and sevenfold.
verses.little_bible: Lamech said that if God protected Cain, then he wanted even more protection for himself. He was making himself bigger than God's rules.
verses.little_reader_adaptation: Lamech thinks he deserves even more than God gave Cain.
verses.meaning: Lamech twisted God's gracious protection of Cain into a demand for greater revenge. He used God's mercy as justification for his own pride — a warning against using God's grace as a licence for sin.
verses.memory_phrase: God's grace is not an excuse for pride.
verses.prayer: God, help me receive Your kindness with a humble heart, not use it to do whatever I want.
verses.discussion_question: Lamech thought he deserved even more than Cain. Have you ever felt like you deserved more than someone else? What did you do with that feeling?
verses.family_discussion: Grace can be misused — Lamech took God's mercy as permission to be worse. How do we help children understand that grace leads to gratitude and humility, not entitlement?
verses.do_it_today: Think of something kind someone has done for you. Say thank you to them today.
verses.illustration_prompt: Lamech standing tall with a raised fist against a stormy sky, while below him small figures look on quietly. Warm storybook watercolor style.

verses.kjv: And Adam knew his wife again; and she bare a son, and called his name Seth: For God, said she, hath appointed me another seed instead of Abel, whom Cain slew.
verses.little_bible: Adam and Eve had another son and named him Seth. Eve said, 'God has given me a new son to take the place of Abel.' God was still keeping His promises.
verses.little_reader_adaptation: Eve has a new son called Seth. God gives new hope after sadness.
verses.meaning: Seth's birth is a moment of grace and hope in a dark chapter. Eve recognised God's hand in providing new life — God was not done with His story of redemption.
verses.memory_phrase: God gives new hope after sadness.
verses.prayer: God, thank You that You always bring hope, even after sad things happen.
verses.discussion_question: Eve said Seth was a gift from God after all the sadness. Have you ever felt that God gave you something good after a hard time?
verses.family_discussion: Seth's birth points forward to hope and eventually to Jesus (Luke 3 traces Jesus' line through Seth). How do we help children see that God's story always moves toward hope, even through loss?
verses.do_it_today: Think of one hopeful thing God has given you. Write it down, draw it, or tell someone about it.
verses.illustration_prompt: Eve cradling a newborn baby named Seth, her face full of tearful joy; soft golden light fills the scene. Warm storybook watercolor style.

verses.kjv: And to Seth, to him also there was born a son; and he called his name Enos: then began men to call upon the name of the LORD.
verses.little_bible: Seth had a son named Enos. And it was around this time that people began to call on God's name again — to pray and seek Him.
verses.little_reader_adaptation: Seth has a son named Enos. People begin to call on God again.
verses.meaning: The chapter ends with a note of hope: people began to seek God again. After the darkness of Cain's line, Seth's line turned back to God. Worship, prayer, and calling on God's name are the beginning of restoration.
verses.memory_phrase: Always call on the name of the LORD.
verses.prayer: God, I call on Your name today. I want to know You and talk to You every day.
verses.discussion_question: What does it mean to 'call on God's name'? How do you talk to God?
verses.family_discussion: After so much darkness in this chapter, the closing note is people turning back to God. How does your family cultivate the habit of calling on God together — in prayer, worship, or Scripture reading?
verses.do_it_today: Call on God's name right now. You can just say, 'God, I am here and I love You.'
verses.illustration_prompt: A group of people of different ages gathered together under a wide open sky, hands raised or heads bowed in prayer, morning light breaking over the horizon. Warm storybook watercolor style.
